items.title: Estimate MI with the right tool for variable type
items.avoid: Applying Pearson correlation or MI with default settings without checking whether features are discrete, continuous, or mixed.
items.prefer: Use `mutual_info_classif` with `discrete_features` flags, or dedicated discrete MI from contingency tables for categorical data; for continuous data, prefer k-NN or well-tuned binning with enough samples per bin.
items.why: Mis-specified estimators inflate or deflate scores, leading to wrong feature rankings.
items.example: Pass `discrete_features=[0, 2]` for one-hot and ID columns in a mixed numeric table before calling `mutual_info_classif`.

items.title: Check redundancy among selected features
items.avoid: Keeping the top-k features by I(X_i; Y) without checking pairwise I(X_i; X_j).
items.prefer: Greedy selection: add features with high I(X_i; Y) only if I(X_i; already_selected) is low, or use mRMR-style criteria balancing relevance and redundancy.
items.why: Two redundant high-MI features waste capacity and can destabilize linear models without adding new label information.
items.example: If I(feature_A; Y) and I(feature_B; Y) are both 0.4 bits but I(A; B) = 0.35 bits, keep one of A or B, not both.

items.title: Report units and normalization
items.avoid: Comparing raw MI scores across problems with different numbers of classes or support sizes without context.
items.prefer: State log base (bits vs nats), use NMI for clustering comparisons, and interpret MI relative to min(H(X), H(Y)).
items.why: A label with H(Y) = 3 bits allows at most 3 bits of MI; a binary label caps MI at 1 bit—absolute numbers are not comparable across tasks.
items.example: When reporting clustering NMI = 0.82, note it is normalized to [0,1], not raw bits.
